EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front was moving through the area. Out ahead of it was plenty of moisture due to the strong southerly flow. This helped to produced showers and storms across the area. The lack of instability meant most storms stayed sub severe. However, one storms was strong enough to bring down some trees, powerlines, and fencing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported large branches and fencing down around the Hampton Inn in Dade City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet, unsettled weather pattern continued over the area with a surface front across central in the morning that lifted north into the afternoon as a warm front as a surface low developed along the boundary in the eastern GOMEX and approached Apalachee Bay. Rainfall and isolated thunderstorms continued across NE FL near the lingering surface front in the morning, then as the front retreated northward toward I-10 into the afternoon, the potential for thunderstorms across NE FL expanded northward |within the warm sector. There was a window for severe storm potential in the afternoon across NE FL where enough instability (1000-1500 J/kg MLCAPE) phased with a 35-40 kt 850 mb jet under about 40-50 kts bulk shear with HREF with STP guidance near 1-1.3  units. These ingredients favor strong wind gust potential in |isolated severe storms during this time frame (40-60 mph gusts) and a low threat of isolated, brief tornadoes with Effective SRH |200 m2/s2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a half of a foot of standing water was reported across County Road 65.

A wave of strong to severe storms developed after 2 pm across NE Florida near and trailing the lifting warm front over an unstable and highly sheared air-mass. These storms expanded in coverage and intensity through 6 pm as they tracked ENE. The combination of high temperatures in the low 80s with dew pts in the upper 60s to low 70s, created high surface based CAPE near 2k J/kg with 40-50 kts of bulk shear. Hail, strong winds and isolated tornadoes impacted the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was observed in Lake City. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region on February 1st, followed by a few days of below normal temperatures. Continental high pressure settled over the Florida peninsula on the night of February 3rd and morning of February 4th, leading to clear nighttime skies and near calm wind which caused ideal radiational cooling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread frost produced light to moderate damage to crops in western Palm Beach County. Crops impacted were: green beans, sweet corn, and sugarcane. Temperatures at the 6 foot level were in the mid 30s, however temperatures just off the ground were at least 5-7 degrees lower due to blackbody radiation in many crop fields in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Veering southerly flow ahead of an approaching cold front helped to advect a rich plume of low-level moisture across South Florida. As showers and thunderstorms associated with the front pushed through the area and stalled, this translated into heavy rainfall and flooding across east coast metro. Also, lightning strikes from one storm struck and damaged the Broward Water Treatment Plant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several videos obtained from the media and public showed flash flooding on NE 23rd Street near Biscayne Blvd with water rising above the bottom line of several building doors, water covering the wheels of cars, and rising to knee-high level. Additional videos show street flooding near Margaret Pace Pack with an estimated two to three inches of water on the ground. Nearby rain gauges measured between 3 to 4 inches of rain in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Veering southerly flow ahead of an approaching cold front helped to advect a rich plume of low-level moisture across South Florida. As showers and thunderstorms associated with the front pushed through the area and stalled, this translated into heavy rainfall and flooding across east coast metro. Also, lightning strikes from one storm struck and damaged the Broward Water Treatment Plant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Broward County 2A Water Treatment Plant located on Copans Road in Pompano Beach. The lightning damaged a number of mechanical, electrical, and instrumentation components including a high service pump, the control modules for two electronically actuated valves, and two programable logic controllers. The failure of this combination of items impeded their ability to fully pressurize the potable water distribution system, resulting in a precautionary boil water order to portions of Broward County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow, increasing swells, and rough surf brought hazardous beach conditions to South Florida Atlantic coast beaches. Two girls were rescued by a rip current in Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Palm Beach Daily News reported that two sisters caught in a rip current were rescued by Palm Beach lifeguards after being spotted in distress just after 11 A.M. on the north end of Midtown Beach in the Town of Palm Beach. Both girls received medical attention as a precaution.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary draped across the area combined with ample low-level moisture has allowed showers to develop and drift over the east coast metro. After repeated rounds of heavy rainfall, flooding materialized across portions of Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video reports from WSVN and WPLG confirm street flooding along US 1 and NE 6th street in Pompano Beach. Water exceeding the curb in some areas and roads completely covered in water, though still passable. Nearby rain gauges measured up to 4 inches of rain in a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front lifted north from central FL during the afternoon as a mid level impulse passed overhead. These features combined with diurnal instability produced a few strong to marginally severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall was 3.40 inches for the day in NW Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system developing over the lower MS River Valley tracked eastward across the Gulf Coast States and brought a couple rounds of severe storms to the local area. The first wave of strong to severe storms approached SE GA midday with strong shear and high low level helicity favoring all severe weather hazards including damaging winds, large hail and tornadoes. This initial band of convection stalled just north of the I-10 corridor and lingered into the afternoon as the first mid level trough lifted NE of the region. This lingering convergent line (outflow boundary) was a focus for continued rounds of severe storms and locally heavy rainfall under strengthening upper level divergence and robust 850 mb winds of 40-50 kts as the parent mid/upper level troughs approached from the west into the evening. The next wave of severe storms developed in the late afternoon and early evening along both the lingering trough/outflow axis across NE FL was well as upstream across south GA as the next strong upper level trough approached, which another round of severe hazards including tornadoes, damaging winds and large hail across SE GA and northeast Florida into the evening. Storms generally weakened a few hours after midnight as stronger dynamics lifted NE of the region, but a few stronger storms during the early morning hours of the 25th produced a brief tornado in Flagler county and hail across our southern tier counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported large hail that dented vehicles about 9 miles north of White Springs along County Road 135.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of strong to severe storms affected the region on April 24th.  Tornadoes, large hail (some to 2 inches in diameter), and widespread damaging winds occurred.  The multiple rounds also caused rainfall amounts in excess of 6 inches in some locations across the tri-state region, resulting in flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 10-year-old boy from Keystone Heights, Florida was caught in a rip current in Daytona Beach. First responders pulled the unresponsive child from the surf and he was taken to a local hospital where he later died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytona Beach Ocean Rescue and local media reported that three children (all siblings) from Keystone Heights, Florida were caught in a rip current in Daytona Beach, near the intersection of Glenview Blvd. and Highway A1A. Lifeguards pulled a 10-year-old boy from the surf unresponsive and administered life saving measures. The child was taken to a local hospital where he later died. The other two siblings were rescued and did not require medical attention.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave disturbance traversing Gulf Coast states produced a mesoscale convective system (MCS) ahead of a slow-moving cold front over the western Gulf that rapidly propagated away from the front across the central and eastern gulf and into West-Central and Southwest Florida during the morning and afternoon hours, producing a tornado and severe wind gust, along with additional wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several lanais, carports, and decks sustained minor damage. A home weather station in             the neighborhood recorded a peak wind gust of 50 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A five unit townhome sustained major damage with a rough estimate over $1 million. The tornado was moving southeast and the worst of the damage east side of the homes. One woman was injured by falling debris inside a home. About 20 other homes in the area had|superficial damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line associated with a mesoscale convective system moved across all of central and south Florida. One storm produced large hail in Orange and Seminole Counties, while a line of severe thunderstorms produced very strong wind gusts along the Treasure Coast, and wind damage in Martin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 2 inch hail in Oviedo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of strong to severe storms affected the region on April 24th.  Tornadoes, large hail (some to 2 inches in diameter), and widespread damaging winds occurred.  The multiple rounds also caused rainfall amounts in excess of 6 inches in some locations across the tri-state region, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 173 and MC Carnley Road was underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed over Flagler County near a stationary front. The storm move quickly southeast along the Flagler-Volusia County line and produced large hail just south of the county line, in Ormond Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported hail from one-half inch to one inch in diameter near the Atlantic coast along the Flagler-Volusia County line, but falling just within the Volusia County side, in Ormond Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm briefly became severe and produced minor wind damage within a small area of Fruitland Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported that wind downed thick trees and that shingle and soffit damage occurred to several homes near Ulrick Street in Fruitland Acres. Photographs were also received of roof damage to a nearby church. The wind damage likely resulted from a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure extended across the region with sea  breezes triggering numerous showers and storms over a moist and diurnally unstable airmass. Sea breeze mergers focused the higher rain chances across inland areas where locally heavy rainfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 3:57 pm, the public reported street flooding near San Marco Blvd and Lasalle Street. Water was up to the curb and spilling over onto sidewalks. At 4 pm, local broadcast media reported street flooding along San Marco Blvd. Water was nearly up to the top of car tires along the street. At 4:10 pm, the public shared a picture via social media of flood water overflowing Craig Creek. Water was also about 8 inches deep in the backyard of a residence in the area. At 4:20 pm, broadcast media reported street flooding along Lasalle Street and Belmonte Ave and the public reported street flooding at Lasalle St and Hendricks Ave. Multiple vehicles were stuck in flood water in San Marco. At 5:10 pm, the public reported street flooding along Landon Avenue. Doppler radar estimated about 1.8 to 2 inches of rainfall accumulation in 1 hour prior to the onset of flood reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave disturbance over Mid-South/Tennessee Valley region and attendant surface low with cold front extending south into the Gulf of Mexico produced a mesoscale convective system (MCS) ahead of the front that moved across the eastern gulf into West-Central and Southwest Florida, spawning a tornado and multiple, separate wind damage reports along with hail. One indirect fatality resulted when a motorist exited a vehicle due to a downed tree and inadvertently contacted a live power line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage from wind and downed trees reported to multiple homes near Spirit Lake and Eagle Lake. Time estimated by radar velocity signature.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple round of showers and thunderstorms ahead of a strong cold front affected the tri-state region during the April 9-10 period.  An initial round of storms on April 9th brought primarily large hail to the region, and then a squall line pushed through on April 10th with primarily damaging winds as well as a few tornadoes, mainly across Florida.  Some flooding was also reported, mainly across Florida where the heaviest rainfall amounts occurred in a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in the vicinity of Crowder Chapel and Hinote Roads near the Shoal River. Several large trees were snapped or uprooted in this area. The tornado then moved east northeast snapping and uprooting several more trees along its path. One property on County Highway 1084 sustained roof damage to the primary residence, a well-built single family home. A large metal building system on the property had large garage doors blown in with subsequent uplift and removal of a substantial portion of the roof. A small garage was also destroyed. A bit further down the highway, another metal building system had a portion of its roof rolled back. The tornado lifted near County Highway 83 just south of Glendale. Damage was consistent with an EF1 tornado with maximum estimated winds around 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple round of showers and thunderstorms ahead of a strong cold front affected the tri-state region during the April 9-10 period.  An initial round of storms on April 9th brought primarily large hail to the region, and then a squall line pushed through on April 10th with primarily damaging winds as well as a few tornadoes, mainly across Florida.  Some flooding was also reported, mainly across Florida where the heaviest rainfall amounts occurred in a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado came ashore near Laguna Beach and heavily damaged a convenience store and a home. This was where the heaviest damage occurred. The convenience store roof was completely torn off of the building and lofted into a nearby house. The concrete block exterior walls were also partially collapsed. It appeared that at least some effort had been made to strap the roof to the walls of the structure, making this damage reflective of an EF2 rating with maximum winds of 125 mph. Behind the convenience store, a house was heavily damaged with portions of the roof missing, but most of the damage to this home came from the roof of the store landing on it. A projectile piece of wood was observed lodged in the side of another nearby home. Several surrounding homes also had minor roof damage, glass breakage, and fencing damaged or torn down. Another home had a carport structure completely collapsed and a wooden shed blown over and rolled into a fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system developing over the lower MS River Valley tracked eastward across the Gulf Coast States and brought a couple rounds of severe storms to the local area. The first wave of strong to severe storms approached SE GA midday with strong shear and high low level helicity favoring all severe weather hazards including damaging winds, large hail and tornadoes. This initial band of convection stalled just north of the I-10 corridor and lingered into the afternoon as the first mid level trough lifted NE of the region. This lingering convergent line (outflow boundary) was a focus for continued rounds of severe storms and locally heavy rainfall under strengthening upper level divergence and robust 850 mb winds of 40-50 kts as the parent mid/upper level troughs approached from the west into the evening. The next wave of severe storms developed in the late afternoon and early evening along both the lingering trough/outflow axis across NE FL was well as upstream across south GA as the next strong upper level trough approached, which another round of severe hazards including tornadoes, damaging winds and large hail across SE GA and northeast Florida into the evening. Storms generally weakened a few hours after midnight as stronger dynamics lifted NE of the region, but a few stronger storms during the early morning hours of the 25th produced a brief tornado in Flagler county and hail across our southern tier counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel was spotted southwest of Flagler Beach. The location was approximated by the report and radar data.

Some flooding was also reported, mainly across Florida where the heaviest rainfall amounts occurred in a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in the southeast part of Jackson county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system developing over the lower MS River Valley tracked eastward across the Gulf Coast States and brought a couple rounds of severe storms to the local area. The first wave of strong to severe storms approached SE GA midday with strong shear and high low level helicity favoring all severe weather hazards including damaging winds, large hail and tornadoes. This initial band of convection stalled just north of the I-10 corridor and lingered into the afternoon as the first mid level trough lifted NE of the region. This lingering convergent line (outflow boundary) was a focus for continued rounds of severe storms and locally heavy rainfall under strengthening upper level divergence and robust 850 mb winds of 40-50 kts as the parent mid/upper level troughs approached from the west into the evening. The next wave of severe storms developed in the late afternoon and early evening along both the lingering trough/outflow axis across NE FL was well as upstream across south GA as the next strong upper level trough approached, which another round of severe hazards including tornadoes, damaging winds and large hail across SE GA and northeast Florida into the evening. Storms generally weakened a few hours after midnight as stronger dynamics lifted NE of the region, but a few stronger storms during the early morning hours of the 25th produced a brief tornado in Flagler county and hail across our southern tier counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video of up to golf ball size hail fell near 7340 SE County Road 135 in eastern Hamilton County. The hail severely damaged crops in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of strong to severe storms affected the region on April 24th.  Tornadoes, large hail (some to 2 inches in diameter), and widespread damaging winds occurred.  The multiple rounds also caused rainfall amounts in excess of 6 inches in some locations across the tri-state region, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down near the Jefferson and Madison county border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active Spring storm season continued with another round of severe storms Friday night into Saturday. A strong storm system moved across the area. Ahead of this system, a very moist and unstable airmass developed, setting the stage for widespread severe storm development. Very cold temperatures in the mid levels of the atmosphere allowing very large hail to develop, including the largest hail stone ever recorded in Baldwin County. Along with the very large hail, damaging winds were also widespread.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front settled across southeast Georgia and lingered across northeast Florida. Diurnal instability combined with a moist airmass triggered scattered showers and isolated storms along the meandering front, some of which produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported along County Road 90 at the Forest Park Street intersection. The dirt road was completely covered in shallow water, but it was still passable by vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical low was approaching the FL panhandle from the Gulf of Mexico with deep tropical moisture on the east side of the system overspreading the local forecast area under SSW flow. Showers and storms developed west to east through the day with storm enhancement toward the I-95 corridor and east coast where an east coast sea breeze drifted inland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broadcast media partner shared a social media video of about 6 inches of flood water over Cordova Street near Valencia Street in historic St. Augustine.

A saturated tropical airmass (PWAT over 2 inches) combined with diurnal instability and fueled locally heavy rainfall in showers and storms that formed near a meandering trough axis across north Florida and sea breezes. Weak storm motion enabled locally heavy rainfall to form and train over areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWS mesonet site measured 3.58 inches of rainfall in the past 6 hours. About 2.07 inches fell in the past hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak east-west oriented trough extended across NE Florida and served as a focus for localized heavy rainfall under a saturated airmass (PWAT over 2 inches). Slow storm motion and PVA in the afternoon enhanced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Naval Station Mayport measured 2.62 inches of rainfall since 7 am.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clearwater Fire Rescue reports that an individual was struck by lightning in Clearwater sustaining minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe squall line moved across the area during the afternoon and evening hours, producing mainly wind damage with gusts of 40-60 mph. A strong shortwave moved  across the Southeast region which drove a mature squall line toward the local area. Steep mid level lapse rates due to drier air aloft and strong low level winds created high DCAPE values which combined to produce strong wind gusts as the broken squall line moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported a tree was blown down near the intersection of County Road 137 and 216th Street. The time of damage was based on radar imagery. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in storm data.

Major structural damage was not observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two part severe weather outbreak impacted the northern Gulf Coast. An early morning squall line brought wind damage to numerous locations. The second round of severe storms in the afternoon and early evening resulted in very large hail across potions of inland southwest and south central AL, some of which reached baseball size. Additional wind damage occurred as well. A few instances of flash flooding were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valley Road from Highway 90 to Stillwell Blvd was closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed in the wake of a line of thunderstorms across much of the Interstate-4 corridor and within Lake County. These winds resulted in damage to pool screens, fences and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind lasting for approximately three hours occurred in Orange County, with peak observed wind gusts up to 53 mph. Trained spotters and the public reported small to medium sized branches were downed and damage to backyard pool enclosures in Windermere and Ocoee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system developing over the lower MS River Valley tracked eastward across the Gulf Coast States and brought a couple rounds of severe storms to the local area. The first wave of strong to severe storms approached SE GA midday with strong shear and high low level helicity favoring all severe weather hazards including damaging winds, large hail and tornadoes. This initial band of convection stalled just north of the I-10 corridor and lingered into the afternoon as the first mid level trough lifted NE of the region. This lingering convergent line (outflow boundary) was a focus for continued rounds of severe storms and locally heavy rainfall under strengthening upper level divergence and robust 850 mb winds of 40-50 kts as the parent mid/upper level troughs approached from the west into the evening. The next wave of severe storms developed in the late afternoon and early evening along both the lingering trough/outflow axis across NE FL was well as upstream across south GA as the next strong upper level trough approached, which another round of severe hazards including tornadoes, damaging winds and large hail across SE GA and northeast Florida into the evening. Storms generally weakened a few hours after midnight as stronger dynamics lifted NE of the region, but a few stronger storms during the early morning hours of the 25th produced a brief tornado in Flagler county and hail across our southern tier counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a home at 2355 NW 99th Avenue in Jasper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active Spring storm season continued with another round of severe storms Friday night into Saturday. A strong storm system moved across the area. Ahead of this system, a very moist and unstable airmass developed, setting the stage for widespread severe storm development. Very cold temperatures in the mid levels of the atmosphere allowing very large hail to develop, including the largest hail stone ever recorded in Baldwin County. Along with the very large hail, damaging winds were also widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road flooded at Green Acres Blvd and Green Acres Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooded streets were reported across the westside of Gainesville including the following areas: SW 45th Street, SW 194th Street and around the 16000 block of NW 143rd Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of strong storms formed near the I-10 corridor during the early morning hours and tracked southward across northeast Florida through mid afternoon. Steep mid level lapse rates in the 7-8 C/km and cold temperatures aloft produced marginally severe hail, rainfall rates of 2+ inches per hour, and gusty winds in the 40-60 mph range over a unstable early morning airmass with surface CAPEs of 2000-2500 J/kg across NE FL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple round of showers and thunderstorms ahead of a strong cold front affected the tri-state region during the April 9-10 period.  An initial round of storms on April 9th brought primarily large hail to the region, and then a squall line pushed through on April 10th with primarily damaging winds as well as a few tornadoes, mainly across Florida.  Some flooding was also reported, mainly across Florida where the heaviest rainfall amounts occurred in a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oak Ave was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man swimming at a Cape Canaveral beach went missing during a period of strong rip currents and rough surf. Brevard County Ocean Rescue presumed the man drowned due to a rip current.

The search was called off the following day and man was presumed drowned. Near the time the man went missing, his nephew was rescued from the water by a surfer.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jens Ave flooded between Baldwin Road and SR 390.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms moved across during the early morning hours. One of the storms produced large hail.
Strong thunderstorms moved across during the early morning hours. One of the storms produced large hail.

A strong cold front moved through the area during the afternoon hours of Feb 15th.  The airmass ahead of the front was relatively unstable, and shear was very strong.  The result was a round of strong to severe storms across the tri-state area with damaging winds and a few tornadoes, including a strong EF2 tornado in Early county, GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across all lanes of Highway 71.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the western Atlantic waters allowed for light easterly wind flow to set up across South FL. With the light flow in place, waterspouts were able to develop across the Atlantic waters. In fact, one even moved onshore. The Atlantic sea breeze also was able push westward across South FL while the Gulf sea breeze remained pinned across the west coast metro areas. This allowed for strong showers and storms to develop during the afternoon hours, including over the Gulf and Atlantic waters due to outflow boundaries. Strong wind gusts, heavy rainfall, flooding, and frequent lightning were the main hazards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed reports of street flooding around east Naples, with water getting into the front yards of homes. Rain gauges in the area measured between 3 to 4 inches of rain in about 2 hours during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary at the surface continued to work its way southward across the Florida peninsula. With deep southerly to southwesterly flow in place, rich moisture continued to advect into South Florida. This allowed for scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across interior sections. Storm organization was multi-cellular with numerous outflow boundaries emanating from the sea breeze boundary collisions. Several days of heavy rainfall led to already saturated soils. Multiple reports of flooding were received across South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glades County Emergency Management reported the closure of State Road 29 between State Road 78 and County Road 74 due to sheet flow flooding. Flood waters were approximately 5 inches deep over State Road 29. The road closure and flooding lasted for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering front near the I-10 corridor combined with an upper level trough aloft. These features combined with the sea breezes over a moist and unstable airmass with weak steering flow produced flooding rainfall over parts of Jacksonville and a few pulse severe storms that had wet downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 515 pm, media showed a video on social media of up to 6 inches of water flooding the intersection of Bay Street and Main Street in downtown Jacksonville. At 525 pm, the media relayed a social media video of 6-12 inches of flood water over Park Street near Avondale Avenue in Jacksonville. At 551 pm, the media shared a social media video of Hogan's Creek overflowing it's banks near Bethel Baptist Street and Laura Street. At 553 pm, the public shared a social media video of minor ponding in their yard along Gilmore Street in Riverside. At 554 pm, the public shared another social media post of street flooding in San Marco near Landon and Flagler Avenues. At 556 pm, the public shared a social media photo of their backyard flooding near Little Fishweir Creek by the intersection of Azalea Drive and Hollingworth Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving over the eastern Gulf of Mexico developed a mesoscale convective complex that moved through South Florida.  Environmental parameters were favorable for the development of strong to severe storms, which produced hail and damaging winds across the east coast metro areas of South Florida.||A total of 63,147 customers lost power during this event. Following is the breakdown by county: Palm Beach 33,136, Broward 11,572, Miami-Dade 18,439.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Videos sent by FPL contractors show damage to a building located off of Southern Blvd in Palm Beach County, just west of PBIA.  Damage consisted of roof and the side panels being blown away.  This damage is a result of downburst winds estimated to be around 70- 75 mph range (60 to 65 knots) and not tornadic.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of tourists from Indiana were visiting Cocoa Beach. A 45-year-old man attempted to aid a female member of his group who was struggling to swim after being caught in a rip current. Cocoa Beach Fire Department personnel deployed a jet ski, and the man and woman were brought to shore. The man was transported to a local hospital, where he was pronounced dead. The female was treated, and she survived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 23-year-old female and 26-year-old male drowned after being caught in a rip current at Melbourne Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two local residents were swimming together at Juan Ponce de Leon Landing Beach in Melbourne Beach when they became trapped in a rip current. The first victim was a 23-year-old female who was unable to be revived at a local hospital. The second victim was a 26-year-old male who disappeared beneath the water. His body was recovered the following afternoon a few miles to the north of where he went missing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter-sized hail near the intersection of NW 164th St and NW 87th Ave near Miami Lakes. Time estimated based on time of report and radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving over the eastern Gulf of Mexico developed a mesoscale convective complex that moved through South Florida.  Environmental parameters were favorable for the development of strong to severe storms, which produced hail and damaging winds across the east coast metro areas of South Florida.||A total of 63,147 customers lost power during this event. Following is the breakdown by county: Palm Beach 33,136, Broward 11,572, Miami-Dade 18,439.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 64 mph/56 knots was recorded at WxFlow site XBOC at 3:41 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample moisture in place allowed for showers and thunderstorms to develop along both the Gulf and Atlantic sea breezes. Numerous boundary collisions from this activity produced a funnel cloud over Miami.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pilot reported a funnel cloud associated with a gusty shower on approach to Miami International Airport (KMIA).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms cause wind damage in Levy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down blocking CR 335 near NE 160th Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced straight-line winds in Cocoa, which resulted in damage to two homes, in addition to tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny sized hail at the US Tennis Association National Campus in Lake Nona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 67-year-old man died and a 24-year-old man was injured after being caught in a rip current in Daytona Beach Shores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 67-year-old man, on vacation from Missouri, was caught in a rip current at an unguarded beach near the 2200 block of South Atlantic Avenue in Daytona Beach Shores. Bystanders pulled the unconscious man from the water and administered CPR as Volusia County Ocean Rescue responded to the scene. The man was taken to a local hospital where he later died. The 24-year-old grandson of the victim, who was also caught in the rip current, was treated on scene for exhaustion and released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms produce severe wind gust near Fort Myers and tree damage near Sarasota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ft. Myers-Page Field Airport reported a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active Spring storm season continued with another round of severe storms Friday night into Saturday. A strong storm system moved across the area. Ahead of this system, a very moist and unstable airmass developed, setting the stage for widespread severe storm development. Very cold temperatures in the mid levels of the atmosphere allowing very large hail to develop, including the largest hail stone ever recorded in Baldwin County. Along with the very large hail, damaging winds were also widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds tore the roof off the Emerald Republic Brewing Company.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms produce severe wind gust near Fort Myers and tree damage near Sarasota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media shared images on social media of large oak tree branches snapped in Sarasota, along with downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving afternoon convection produces a severe wind gust in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lakeland Linder International Airport (KLAL) reported a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that moved through Fruitland Park resulted in several downed trees near the western shore of Lake Griffin. Another thunderstorm produced very minor, localized wind damage in Oviedo, mainly to a few small trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake County Emergency Management reported several trees downed by thunderstorm winds in Fruitland Park. The tree damage occurred along Sail Fish Avenue, Robin Drive, and Old Hickory Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that moved through Fruitland Park resulted in several downed trees near the western shore of Lake Griffin. Another thunderstorm produced very minor, localized wind damage in Oviedo, mainly to a few small trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A resident in Oviedo near the intersection of County Road 426 and Lee Avenue reported several damaged fences, flagpoles, a few missing shingles on a couple of homes, and damage to a couple of small trees. Photos were provided, which were consistent with localized wind gusts of 50-55 mph. A strong thunderstorm crossed the area at the time of the reported damage, which likely produced a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms that developed in Brevard County produced severe wind gusts near Cape Canaveral. One of the storms produced a brief, weak tornado as it moved onshore from the Indian River and affected the Preserve Point subdivision in northwest Merritt Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Air Force wind tower 0001 measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph from the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms that developed in Brevard County produced severe wind gusts near Cape Canaveral. One of the storms produced a brief, weak tornado as it moved onshore from the Indian River and affected the Preserve Point subdivision in northwest Merritt Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms that developed in Brevard County produced severe wind gusts near Cape Canaveral. One of the storms produced a brief, weak tornado as it moved onshore from the Indian River and affected the Preserve Point subdivision in northwest Merritt Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A resident of the Preserve Pointe subdivision on Merritt Island, just south of the NASA Causeway and west of State Road 3, observed trees being uprooted, fences destroyed and solar panels ripping off of neighbors' roofs as a severe thunderstorms passed by. The eyewitness report along with videos and photos confirmed that a brief, weak (EF-0) tornado likely developed within the thunderstorm and produced additional scattered, (mainly tree) damage northeastward to Pine Island Road. The circulation likely initially reached the ground within the Pine Island Conservation Area to the southwest of Preserve Pointe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple round of showers and thunderstorms ahead of a strong cold front affected the tri-state region during the April 9-10 period.  An initial round of storms on April 9th brought primarily large hail to the region, and then a squall line pushed through on April 10th with primarily damaging winds as well as a few tornadoes, mainly across Florida.  Some flooding was also reported, mainly across Florida where the heaviest rainfall amounts occurred in a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was widespread across Lynn Haven Parkway as a line of storms pushed through the area. Despite there being damage well south of the estimated path, much of this damage is estimated to come from straight line winds associated with the squall line. Roof damage to Haney Technical Center and a blown over semi on the Parkway were observed and consistent with these straight line winds. Damage just south of 14th street near the estimated path, including the greatest damage to Grease Pro Express, was found to be consistent with that of a strong EF1 tornado. At that location, the roof was lifted upwards and displaced/rotated about 30 degrees. Similar, but less intense damage, was found at homes near the Grease Pro Express. Outside of this location, minor roof damage was observed at many locations, but none of this damage was consistent with a tornado and the estimated duration of the tornado was very brief, 3 minutes at most. Maximum winds were estimated to be 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple round of showers and thunderstorms ahead of a strong cold front affected the tri-state region during the April 9-10 period. Some flooding was also reported, mainly across Florida where the heaviest rainfall amounts occurred in a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down throughout Liberty county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pre-frontal squall line moved across the area with isolated severe storms. Shear was 40-60 kts with surface based CAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 touched down briefly NE of Trenton in Gilchrist county near the Gilchrist-Alachua county line where a barn fell onto a tractor. The tornado likely lifted as it the parent supercell tracked northeast toward the city of Alachua, in Alachua county. Here, the tornado touched down again and damaged a carport roof which was peeled back. Trees were also damaged and fell in this location, one of which fell onto a car. Peak winds were estimated at 81 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One fatality and one injury occurred due to rip currents.  Beach flags were double red for the entire day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One fatality and one injury occurred due to rip currents. The fatality was due to a rescue attempt of another person in distress.  Beach flags were double red for the entire day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong onshore flow wind event (local Nor'Easter) produced non-convective wind gusts of 40-50 mph through the day which caused some minor wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds caused minor wind damage in Flagler Beach. Around 1:15 pm local time, a tree was blown down onto power lines and a home along Audubon Lane. The power lines came down and minor structure damage was reported. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong onshore flow wind event (local Nor'Easter) produced non-convective wind gusts of 40-50 mph through the day which caused some minor wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty gradient winds of 40-50 mph caused a tree to fall down onto power lines at the 5200 block of John Anderson Highway south of Flagler Beach. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data.

Training waves of heavy rainfall occurred which caused minor localized flooding issues, including swollen ditches. A lone severe storm developed in the evening across Marion county which produced some wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring cold front moved through the tri-state region.  A squall line preceded the front with scattered reports of severe weather, including a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down just south of the Northwest Florida Beaches International Airport with most damage occurring on the airport property. A few small pine trees were snapped on Johnny Reeves Road and Venture Crossings Blvd. A ground-level metal sign was bent over 90 degrees at the intersection of Venture Crossings Blvd. and West Bay Parkway. At the Sheltair FBO, a Citation aircraft spun 90 degrees with the nose damaging an adjacent plane, which was spun 20 degrees. Other smaller planes were shifted further north on the property near an area of hangars. One hangar lost a small section of roof and had the door damaged. Wind entering the hangar did considerable damage to one of the antique cars being stored there. The metal door track punched a hole through the wall of the hangar. Another hangar also suffered minor roof damage. Finally, the KECP ASOS measured a gust to 82 mph as the tornado passed by. The tornado then tracked a couple of miles northeast of the airport property through uninhabited terrain before lifting. Damage along the path, and the measured gust, are consistent with high-end EF0 damage with max winds estimated at 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring cold front moved through the tri-state region.  A squall line preceded the front with scattered reports of severe weather, including a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along the north shore of a small lake near S. Lake Joanna Dr. and Sweet Lane. The tornado travelled east-northeast across the lake, producing EF1 damage to a home at the northeast end of the lake in the 7300 block of S. Lake Joanna Dr, including removal of a back porch canopy roof and causing a sizable opening in the main roof of the home. Debris from this house peppered another nearby home, with two 2x4s puncturing its back porch awning. The tornado crossed east-northeast across Bayou George Dr. On the other side of Bayou George Dr. the tornado hit a PODS storage yard, where PODS were originally in 3-high stacks. Some of the PODS shifted, and one POD unit was tossed. The tornado then lifted. Tornado damage at the home at the east-northeast end of the lake in the 7300 block of S. Lake Joanna Drive is consistent with a tornado of EF1 strength with max winds around 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strengthening easterly to southeasterly flow contributed to building seas and rough surf across the Atlantic waters. This led to hazardous marine and beach conditions to the South Florida Atlantic coast beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple media outlets reported two rip current related fatalities at Miami Beach, near 54th Street and Collins Avenue, on Wednesday, March 31st. Calls for help for two swimmers in distress (a father and his daughter) occurred around 5:45 PM EDT. A good Samaritan who jumped in to help was pulled out of the water unresponsive by rescuers but was pronounced dead at Mount Sinai Hospital. The daughter was rescued, no reports of her injuries, if any. ||As for the father, a search was called off Wednesday night due to rough seas. The search resumed Thursday morning and Miami Beach Ocean Rescue found his body near where he was last seen in the water. Miami Beach Ocean Rescue reported at least 40 rip current rescues throughout the day on Wednesday.||The location of the drownings was in between two Miami Beach Ocean Rescue lifeguard stations located about 0.8 miles apart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across Leesburg, producing hail and localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported numerous 1 inch and larger tree branches downed throughout The Plantation at Leesburg community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 89 acre wildfire destroyed three homes and two vehicles in Port Saint Lucie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three homes and two vehicles were destroyed by a wildfire in Port Saint Lucie near the intersection of Dyer Road and Silver Oak Drive. The wildfire was 89 acres at its largest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty east winds and dry low level conditions promoted fire growth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oscar Aston fire in St. Johns county was near Oscar Aston Road and the Watson Island State Forest. Fire crews responded around 2 pm when the fire was near 80 acres. By 9 pm, the fire had burned up to 675 acres and was 75 percent contained. There was not immediate threat to structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy easterly surface and transport winds continued to create high dispersion over a dry low level airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marion county reported a 4 acre wildfire in Dunnellon around 430 pm. Two homes were impacted at the time. Evacuations of 5-7 other homes occurred. By 830 pm, the fire grew to 80 acres and was 90% contained. Two mobiles homes and 4 outbuildings were destroyed by the fire. Three homes were partially damaged. There were 25 homes were evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent easterly winds combined with elevated surf over the weekend created a High Rip Current risk along local NE FL beaches and resulted in numerous ocean rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flagler Beach Ocean Rescue pulled 4 people from the ocean who were caught in a rip current at South 6th Street in Flagler Beach. There were no injuries and medical attention was not needed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent easterly winds combined with elevated surf over the weekend created a High Rip Current risk along local NE FL beaches and resulted in numerous ocean rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swimmer was pulled to safety by Flagler Beach Ocean Rescue in Central Flagler Beach. Medial treatment was not needed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent easterly winds combined with elevated surf over the weekend created a High Rip Current risk along local NE FL beaches and resulted in numerous ocean rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four people were rescued from the surf by at least 7 lifeguards near Ocean Hammock Park. It took 20 minutes to rescue everyone. One person needed medical attention and was transported to a hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent easterly winds combined with elevated surf over the weekend created a High Rip Current risk along local NE FL beaches and resulted in numerous ocean rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 44 ocean rescues occurred for Jacksonville Beach Lifeguards from May 22 through May 23 due to surf and rip currents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent easterly winds combined with elevated surf over the weekend created a High Rip Current risk along local NE FL beaches and resulted in numerous ocean rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One male drowned due to a rip current. He was trying to save his two young children from the rip current. Witnesses were able to create a human-chain and save the children, but were unable to rescue the victim. The drowning occurred at Huguenot Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent easterly winds combined with elevated surf over the weekend created a High Rip Current risk along local NE FL beaches and resulted in numerous ocean rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A male went into cardiac arrest and died as a result of being caught in a rip current. Many people tried to help the victim return to shore before rescuers arrived, but they, too, were caught in the rip current. Lifeguards rescued the victim, but he was later pronouced dead at the hospital. Others at the incident were rescued from rip currents and treated by on-scene paramedics.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east with strong to severe pre-frontal storms that impacted mainly the I-10 corridor of NE FL during the early to mid afternoon hours where deep shear coincided with elevated surface instability.  Decent mid level lapse rates around 6.5 C/km (per JAX 12Z morning sounding) and surface based cape levels between 400-800 J/kg phased under low level shear of around 30 knots with deeper shear values over 60 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A motel exterior soffit near US Highway 129 and I-10 was damage by winds. Multiple softwood trees were uprooted and a hardwood tree truck snapped along 70th Street. Radar estimated the time of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training convection ahead of a cold front resulted in bands of heavy rain in a short period of time and a couple of reports of flash flooding.  The heaviest amounts of 5 to 8 inches fell in a swath from Franklin county northeastward to Jefferson county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water around 6 inches deep reached the rims of tires near Highway 22 and Cherry Street as well as Bob Little Road due to heavy rainfall of a few inches in a short period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training convection ahead of a cold front resulted in bands of heavy rain in a short period of time and a couple of reports of flash flooding.  The heaviest amounts of 5 to 8 inches fell in a swath from Franklin county northeastward to Jefferson county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 19 north of the railroad bridge was temporarily closed due to flooding from heavy rain of 5-8 inches in a short period of time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree was blown down on Bob Sikes Road near De Funiak Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across the roadway on NE Blueberry Drive in the Ocheesee community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east with strong to severe pre-frontal storms that impacted mainly the I-10 corridor of NE FL during the early to mid afternoon hours where deep shear coincided with elevated surface instability.  Decent mid level lapse rates around 6.5 C/km (per JAX 12Z morning sounding) and surface based cape levels between 400-800 J/kg phased under low level shear of around 30 knots with deeper shear values over 60 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong subtropical high pressure over the western Atlantic contributed to breezy conditions across South Florida. This led to hazardous marine and beach conditions to the South Florida Atlantic coast beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pompano Beach Fire Rescue and Emergency Management reported the drowning of a 50-year-old man while rescuing several swimmers in distress from rip currents at around 5 PM EST at Pompano Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms impacted portions of southwest Georgia and northwest Florida during the late morning and early afternoon hours.  Strong shear resulted in a tornado tracking along the south side of Tallahassee and impacting the Tallahassee airport, causing a parked plane to flip over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado initially touched down on Oscar Harvey Rd., snapping some tree tops. The tornado then did damage consistent with an EF1 rating at Blountstown Highway and Seabs Rd. Several large live oak trees were uprooted and several out buildings were destroyed. Winds peaked around 90 mph in this area. The tornado lifted over the Apalachicola National Forest just east of the property.  Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms impacted portions of southwest Georgia and northwest Florida during the late morning and early afternoon hours.  Strong shear resulted in a tornado tracking along the south side of Tallahassee and impacting the Tallahassee airport, causing a parked plane to flip over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just west of the Tallahassee International Airport. The preliminary touch down point is estimated by radar. Once the tornado reached the airport, it moved a 737 from its parked position, spun a CRJ plane 90 degrees and flipped a small Piper plane upside down. There was also damage to a few hangars with some small percentage of metal roofing uplifted. One hangar had large canvas doors on both sides that were shredded with the support structure for one of the canvas doors wrenched free. A bit further east on the west end of the employee lot, one hardwood tree was uprooted and fell onto two cars. A few pine trees were snapped in the immediate vicinity. This one area had damage consistent with low-end EF-1 damage. The tornado may have lifted briefly as it|tracked east-southeast across sparsely populated areas. It did snap a large branch on a tree near Crawfordville Road and Rehwinkle Drive. There were several thin pines snapped along a half mile stretch of Tram Road just west of Capital Circle SE. Many of these trees were likely weakened by Hurricane Michael. Damage was done to a few small trees along the southern periphery of the Southwood Community, along Shumard Oak Blvd. and Four Oaks Blvd. There were then sporadic instances of damage along Tram Road east of Southwood. Damage was then noted along St. Joe Rd. with several trees snapped and uprooted there. Uprooted trees and minor property damage occurred on Fox Tail Road and Bobby Godwin Lane before the tornado lifted just west the Jefferson County line.  Damage cost was estimated.  Most of the damage cost is due to the small Piper plane that was flipped over and eventually declared a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal line of thunderstorms moved across NE FL during the evening hours through early morning on Feb. 1st ahead of a cold front and upper level trough. Strong shear and marginal instability supported strong wind gusts in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site near TIAA Bank Field measured a wind gust to 63 mph. The time was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level short wave trough over a moist and unstable airmass supported a few strong to severe storms that developed across NE FL south of a Gainesville to St Augustine line in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed near Quail Hollow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level short wave trough over a moist and unstable airmass supported a few strong to severe storms that developed across NE FL south of a Gainesville to St Augustine line in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a power line that ignited a fire and damaged a power line pole in Bulow Woods Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front meandered north of the local area with zonal flow aloft. A dominant west coast sea breeze regime focused higher thunderstorm potential across the eastern portion of NE FL in the afternoon and evening hours, with a few pulse severe storms producing lightning and some wind damage near sea breeze and outflow boundary mergers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a pine tree, which fell onto a home. The home then caught fire. The event was at 10733 Orchard Walk Place near Hartley Road in Mandarin. The time of the event was based on radar and lightning data. The cost of the damage was unknown but it was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data. The estimate is likely underestimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front edging south across SE Georgia combined with a passing mid level short wave trough triggered a few strong to isolated severe storms over a moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged a few boat house roofs on the St. Johns River near Orangedale, a few mile north of the Shands Bridge. Patio furniture was blown into the river. The cost of damage was estimated for inclusion of the damage in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms moved from the mainland, across north Merritt Island, to the Atlantic during the early evening. One of the thunderstorms briefly became severe and produced winds over 50 knots at the Kennedy Space Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 mph was observed at the NASA Landing Strip at the Kennedy Space Center on north Merritt Island as a thunderstorm became locally severe for a brief time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa tracked NNE across the western tier of the local forecast area through the day on Wed, July 7th. The storm made landfall along the FL Big Bend coast. The main impacts from TS Elsa for the local area included 3 confirmed tornadoes, downed trees due to gusty winds, and heavy rainfall which caused river flooding. There was one loss of life due to a tree falling onto a car. Peak rainfall amount was near 7.2 inches in Alachua county and the peak recorded wind gust in a squall was 58 mph in Jekyll Island GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer measured storm total rainfall of 5.99  inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prefrontal squall line passed across northeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening hours. A few bowing segments developed within the broader line of thunderstorms and one of these features generated a brief tornado as it tracked east-southeastward around 30 MPH across the Starke FL area. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for the area with potential for brief and isolated tornadoes.||The synoptic environment had strong shear, but an inversion near 500 mb limited updraft stretching and kept convection relatively shallow as the squall line moved across the area. During the late afternoon, a bowing segment coincided with a nose of low level moisture (dew pt rose to near 70 deg) as the convection moved east across Bradford county near Starke. This is where there was enough low level instability and shear to support a mesovortex tornado along the leading edge of the bow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous hard and softwood trees where snapped and some fell onto homes causing structural damage across the ESE of Starke. from Thomson Street to Wilson Road near the Shands Starke Medical Center. One home was shifted off of its foundation due to a large tree falling onto it. Metal roof panels were stripped away from a canopy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front across central Florida on 2/6 lifted northward as a surface low developed along the front in the eastern Gulf of Mexico. The warm front progressed northward to just south of the I-10 corridor during the night 2/6 into the early morning hours of 2/7 as the surface low track east along the front and then offshore of the local Atlantic coast early in the morning 2/7. Very strong shear was in place across the area with elevated instability with mid level lapse rates 7-7.5 deg C/km.  Some wind damage with gusts of 40-50 mph was reported as well as hail as large as quarter size. Widespread rainfall of 2-4 inches occurred in a couple of swaths across NE FL within 24 hrs which produced some localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 3.62 inches of rainfall in the Glynea area of Jacksonville since midnight on 2/6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a brief, weak tornado (landspout) in Vero Lakes Estates, resulting in minor damage to fences and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on radar, video, and eyewitness reports received from the public, a brief EF-0 tornado (landspout) occurred in Vero Lakes Estates. Peak estimated wind speeds were between 65 and 70 mph. The landspout tornado formed at approximately 1612 LST at 100th Avenue, just north of 77th Street, in Vero Lakes Estates. The tornado traveled a relatively short distance, moving almost due north along 100th Avenue, then dissipated just before reaching 79th Street at approximately 1613 LST. Several properties sustained minor fence damage and many had unsecured backyard items scattered around. There was also minor tree damage along the street.

Nearby outbuildings were not damaged. A few pine trees along the driveway of the property were snapped. Further north on Wheeler lane, a double wide manufactured home has some roof damage. Other trees were uprooted on Pineoaks Lane and Akers Trail. The damage was consistent with a high end EF0 with max estimated winds around 85 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF0 tornado came onshore just west of Pier Park shortly after 2pm CT on February 15th. Minor damage to structures near the beach was noted, along with damage to poles, bleachers, and fencing at Frank Brown Park before the tornado dissipated. The tornado is estimated to have had peak winds around 85 mph and less than 50 yards wide. Additional damage to the east of the path was likely due to damaging straight line winds from the rear flank downdraft of the parent supercell. Winds in this area may have been as high as 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prefrontal squall line passed across northeast Florida during the afternoon and early evening hours. A few bowing segments developed within the broader line of thunderstorms and one of these features generated a brief tornado as it tracked east-southeastward around 30 MPH across the Starke FL area. A Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for the area with potential for brief and isolated tornadoes.||The synoptic environment had strong shear, but an inversion near 500 mb limited updraft stretching and kept convection relatively shallow as the squall line moved across the area. During the late afternoon, a bowing segment coincided with a nose of low level moisture (dew pt rose to near 70 deg) as the convection moved east across Bradford county near Starke. This is where there was enough low level instability and shear to support a mesovortex tornado along the leading edge of the bow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in Wellborn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms damage structure attached to home near Ellenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front across SE Georgia brought moist, deep layer WSW flow across NE Florida. An upper level trough axis extended from the SE Atlantic coast to across the FL panhandle. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was stranded in flood water near the intersection of Flagler Ave and Land Ave in San Marco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front across central Florida on 2/6 lifted northward as a surface low developed along the front in the eastern Gulf of Mexico. The warm front progressed northward to just south of the I-10 corridor during the night 2/6 into the early morning hours of 2/7 as the surface low track east along the front and then offshore of the local Atlantic coast early in the morning 2/7. Very strong shear was in place across the area with elevated instability with mid level lapse rates 7-7.5 deg C/km.  Some wind damage with gusts of 40-50 mph was reported as well as hail as large as quarter size. Widespread rainfall of 2-4 inches occurred in a couple of swaths across NE FL within 24 hrs which produced some localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Alachua.

One of these storms took on brief supercell |characteristics with strong rotation evident on radar as it came |into Redington Beach. A NWS meteorologist was unable to find |damage on the barrier island itself, so it was determined a |waterspout formed over Boca Ciega Bay in Pinellas County, moving |generally northeast. The waterspout then came onshore and caused a|very narrow swath of EF-0 damage along Boca Ciega Point Blvd N. |The tornado then tracked north-northeast and caused roof damage to|the Ridgeview apartment complex. The peak intensity of 75 mph was|determined by the roof decking damage here... The tornado then |quickly dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across SE GA from the NE in the afternoon and became nearly stationary across the local forecast area near the GA/FL line. Aloft, upper level forcing rotated across the area within the base of an 500mb trough. The combination of diurnal heating, frontal convergence, and upper energy fueled numerous strong to severe storms across the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Galewind Lane and blocked a residence in Arlington. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa tracked NNE across the western tier of the local forecast area through the day on Wed, July 7th. The storm made landfall along the FL Big Bend coast. The main impacts from TS Elsa for the local area included 3 confirmed tornadoes, downed trees due to gusty winds, and heavy rainfall which caused river flooding. There was one loss of life due to a tree falling onto a car. Peak rainfall amount was near 7.2 inches in Alachua county and the peak recorded wind gust in a squall was 58 mph in Jekyll Island GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 5:10 pm EDT, the broadcast media reported trees and power lines down across the road at the intersection of University Blvd and Interstate 95, which caused power outages. The cost of damage was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa tracked NNE across the western tier of the local forecast area through the day on Wed, July 7th. The storm made landfall along the FL Big Bend coast. The main impacts from TS Elsa for the local area included 3 confirmed tornadoes, downed trees due to gusty winds, and heavy rainfall which caused river flooding. There was one loss of life due to a tree falling onto a car. Peak rainfall amount was near 7.2 inches in Alachua county and the peak recorded wind gust in a squall was 58 mph in Jekyll Island GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts knocked down up to 30 live oak trees and power lines in Dowling Park. The trees were uprooted and snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa tracked NNE across the western tier of the local forecast area through the day on Wed, July 7th. The storm made landfall along the FL Big Bend coast. There was one loss of life due to a tree falling onto a car. Peak rainfall amount was near 7.2 inches in Alachua county and the peak recorded wind gust in a squall was 58 mph in Jekyll Island GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF0 tornado touchdown occurred in an outer band of Tropical Storm Elsa during the early morning hours, and impacted the property at 369 Raven Road, south of Lake City, in Columbia county. Emergency Management conducted a storm survey and observed soft and hard wood trees snapped and broken. A back porch with a metal roof was lifted and peeled back and adjoining plywood walls collapsed. Debris was strewn across the property. Maximum peak winds were estimated at 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced straight-line winds in Cocoa, which resulted in damage to two homes, in addition to tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds, estimated between 65-70 mph, occurred at the Cocoa Bay Mobile Home Community around 1335 LST. Based on information provided by Brevard County Emergency Management, Cocoa Fire Department, local news media, and eyewitness reports, the most significant damage occurred to two homes that had sections of their roofs uplifted. Additional damage in the community along Cocoa Bay Blvd. included downed fences, damaged screens and awnings, and large branches downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa tracked NNE across the western tier of the local forecast area through the day on Wed, July 7th. The storm made landfall along the FL Big Bend coast. The main impacts from TS Elsa for the local area included 3 confirmed tornadoes, downed trees due to gusty winds, and heavy rainfall which caused river flooding. There was one loss of life due to a tree falling onto a car. Peak rainfall amount was near 7.2 inches in Alachua county and the peak recorded wind gust in a squall was 58 mph in Jekyll Island GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cyclonic supercell storm within an outer rain band of TS Elsa's east side produced two tornado touchdowns. The first touchdown occurred in south-central Duval County and then over eastern Camden County in SE Georgia. This report will focus on the Duval County damage. A NWS Storm Survey found that the tornado initially touched down along San Ardo Drive, just NE of Beauclerc. Large tree branches were snapped and damage occurred to residences near Goodby's Creek. The tornado continued to track northward through San Jose with EF-1 damage noted along Old King's Road South and Powers Avenue, just west of Baker-Skinner Park where several trees were completely uprooted. One large pine tree was  snapped and fell over a deck at the Heron Walk Apartment Complex. The tornado reached it's peak intensity and width as it crossed Phillips Highway and moved along Bowdendale Avenue where significant damage was noted to several industrial buildings. Damage to the buildings included metal siding ripped off and roofs peeled back, with debris lofted to the top of a nearby radio antenna tower. The tornado crossed Interstate 95 near the Bowden Road interchange, where large tree branches snapped and more roof damage occurred alogn Spring Park Road. The tornado weakened as it impacted University Christian School and then just northeast of Englewood High School, where tree branches were snapped along Daryl Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough east of the MS Valley, with its base over FL, and an embedded shortwave moving across South FL helped a late season cold front push through the region. This in combination with both the Atlantic and Gulf sea breezes developing and interacting with one another along/near the I-95 corridor allowed for ample surface convergence to occur. Environmental parameters were favorable for the development of strong to severe storms, which produced small hail, flooding, and damaging winds across the east coast metro areas of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public sent videos of flooding near Dinner Key Marina and near 27th Ave and Coral Way due to multiple rounds of thunderstorms. Water exceeding curbs and halfway up car tires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man attempted to rescue his grandson who began to struggle in the surf. The grandson survived, but the man succumbed to drowning due to a strong rip current and rough surf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58-year old man from Kissimmee, Florida drowned along the Canaveral National Seashore, at Playalinda Beach. The man noticed his 11-year old grandson struggling to swim and he entered the water to assist. On their way back to shore, the man was swept under the water and disappeared for a few minutes. He then resurfaced, but was unresponsive. CPR was performed but the man died. The drowning was likely a result of a strong rip current, combined with rough surf. The grandson was injured, but survived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system, turned tropical disturbance, moving across the central tropical Atlantic formed into Tropical Storm Elsa on July 1st. Tropical Storm Elsa gradually continued westward over the next few days before turning northwestward and towards the FL peninsula on July 4th and 5th. The center passed well to the west of South FL, in the eastern Gulf of Mexico, during the day of July 6th, with a minimum central pressure of 1000 mb and maximum sustained winds of 70 mph. For the most part, as Tropical Storm Elsa continued northward and towards the FL Panhandle, it maintained tropical storm intensity, though briefly achieved weak hurricane status late July 6th before decreasing back to tropical storm intensity early July 7th . Lowest surface pressure recorded over South Florida Atlantic Coast was 1012.4 at Naples Municipal Airport and maximum sustained winds of 37 mph over land at University of Miami Health System on July 6th. Recorded Tropical Storm force wind gusts were measured across South Florida ranging from 39 to 49 mph.||Although the effects from Tropical Storm Elsa were generally minor for wind and rainfall, wind gusts of tropical storm force were measured across coastal/metro locations of Palm Beach, Broward, Miami-Dade, and Collier counties. Rainfall amounts ranged from around 3.0 inches to almost 5 inches across Glades and Collier counties. A peak storm surge of 2.10 feet was observed at Naples Pier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As TS Elsa moved westward near the FL Straits and then northward once in the eastern Gulf of Mexico, outer rain bands produced maximum sustained winds generally between 25-35 mph. An earlier rain band moved across Palm Beach County, with peak wind gusts 39 mph/34 knots recorded by the mesonet (LOHF1) at Loxahatchee. Overall wind impacts were minor.

Aloft, the axis of Atlantic surface ridging remained stretched across south FL while a potent trough across the Great Lakes region was drove a broad longwave trough|across the eastern third of the nation. The added forcing from these features combined with high moisture and instability with speed shear triggered a few strong to pulse severe storms across the local area in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system, turned tropical disturbance, moving across the central tropical Atlantic formed into Tropical Storm Elsa on July 1st. Tropical Storm Elsa gradually continued westward over the next few days before turning northwestward and towards the FL peninsula on July 4th and 5th. The center passed well to the west of South FL, in the eastern Gulf of Mexico, during the day of July 6th, with a minimum central pressure of 1000 mb and maximum sustained winds of 70 mph. For the most part, as Tropical Storm Elsa continued northward and towards the FL Panhandle, it maintained tropical storm intensity, though briefly achieved weak hurricane status late July 6th before decreasing back to tropical storm intensity early July 7th . Lowest surface pressure recorded over South Florida Atlantic Coast was 1012.4 at Naples Municipal Airport and maximum sustained winds of 37 mph over land at University of Miami Health System on July 6th. Recorded Tropical Storm force wind gusts were measured across South Florida ranging from 39 to 49 mph.||Although the effects from Tropical Storm Elsa were generally minor for wind and rainfall, wind gusts of tropical storm force were measured across coastal/metro locations of Palm Beach, Broward, Miami-Dade, and Collier counties. Rainfall amounts ranged from around 3.0 inches to almost 5 inches across Glades and Collier counties. A peak storm surge of 2.10 feet was observed at Naples Pier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As TS Elsa moved westward near the FL Straits and then northward once in the eastern Gulf of Mexico, outer rain bands produced maximum sustained winds generally between 25-35 mph. An earlier rain band moved across metro Miami-Dade County, with peak wind gusts of 40 mph/35 knots recorded by the mesonet at UM Health System. Overall wind impacts were minor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal boundary slowly moved southward across the Lake Region of South FL. This in combination with abundant moisture, cloud erosion, and strengthening deep layer shear near 35-45 kts allowed for strong to severe storms to develop across the region, especially inland areas and the Lake Okeechobee Region. Strong winds and heavy rain were recorded, though hail was the main hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported hail up to quarter size (1.00 inch) on Highway 29 in Hendry County between Labelle and Felda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions across South Florida, especially western interior sections, allowed a wildfire to burn in Collier County for over a week. The fire quickly spread and lingered due to lack of rain and gusty winds.  Smoke produced very low to no visibility in the area periodically over the course of the fire's lifespan. Multiple homes damaged, some destroyed. Voluntary evacuation orders were in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of several road closures, including areas on Everglades Blvd, Golden Gate Blvd, and I-75/Alligator Alley, due to smoke from the 14th Avenue Wildfire. The fire caused the loss of two homes, an additional two sustaining damage, and multiple outbuildings damaged or destroyed. The source of the fire is unknown. The total acreage of the fire was 1,500 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East northeasterly winds increased through the morning as high pressure wedged southward and a coastal trough developed. A weakness aloft remained over the area, with cooler temperatures aloft (-8.5 to -9.5C at 500mb) and aided in shower/thunderstorm development. Storms also focused along and south of a frontal boundary draped across the area. Heavy rainfall occurred on the south side of the meandering front with a few strong storms given elevated surface instability across NE FL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front stalls over Central Florida that helped to cool the atmosphere aloft. That along with the extra moisture and lift helped to enhance the sea breeze convection in the afternoon and evening hours. This brought the right environment to bring hail across parts of West Central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public shared a picture on social media with 1.25 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong showers and storms sparked from both the Gulf and Atlantic sea breezes produced heavy rainfall, hail, and strong winds across inland sections of South FL during the afternoon hours.
